name: Sociology
description: An introduction to the scientific study of society — culture, socialization, social structures, inequality, race, gender, deviance, institutions, and social change.

modules.title: Introduction to Sociology
modules.objectives: Define sociology and the sociological imagination; compare functionalism, conflict theory, and symbolic interactionism.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 1: An Introduction to Sociology
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Sociology #1 — What Is Sociology?
modules.assignment: Write a one-page reflection applying C. Wright Mills's sociological imagination to a personal experience. How does your private situation connect to broader social forces?

modules.title: Research Methods in Sociology
modules.objectives: Distinguish between quantitative and qualitative research methods; explain ethical considerations in sociological research.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 2: Sociological Research
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Research Methods in Social Science
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Sociology #4 — Sociological Research Methods
modules.assignment: Design a brief research proposal on a social topic of your choice. Identify your research question, choose a method (survey, interview, or ethnography), and address one ethical consideration.

modules.title: Culture
modules.objectives: Define culture and its components (norms, values, symbols, beliefs); distinguish subcultures and countercultures; explain cultural change.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 3: Culture
modules.assignment: Identify a subculture you belong to or are familiar with. Describe three norms, two values, and two symbols unique to that subculture and explain how they differ from mainstream culture.

modules.title: Socialization
modules.objectives: Identify primary agents of socialization; explain how identity is formed; evaluate nature vs. nurture in human development; describe life-course socialization.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 5: Socialization
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Socialization and the Self
modules.assignment: Reflect on three agents of socialization that most shaped your own identity (e.g., family, school, media, peers). For each, give a specific example of a value or behavior you learned and explain whether it was intentional or unintentional.

modules.title: Social Interaction & Groups
modules.objectives: Define status and role; compare primary and secondary groups; explain formal organizations and bureaucracy using sociological concepts.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 6: Groups and Organization
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Social Groups and Organizations
modules.assignment: Map your own social network: list two primary groups and two secondary groups you belong to. For each, identify your status, your role, and one way that group influences your behavior.

modules.title: Deviance & Social Control
modules.objectives: Explain how deviance is socially defined; apply strain theory, labeling theory, and social control theory; describe formal and informal social control.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 7: Deviance, Crime, and Social Control
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Deviance and Social Control
modules.assignment: Apply labeling theory to a real or hypothetical example of someone labeled 'deviant.' Explain how the label affected that person's identity and life chances, using sociological vocabulary.

modules.title: Social Stratification
modules.objectives: Compare caste, class, and meritocracy systems; define socioeconomic status; analyze social mobility and structural causes of poverty.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 9: Social Stratification in the United States
modules.assignment: Compare absolute and relative poverty using two real-world examples. Then identify two structural factors (e.g., education access, discrimination) that make it difficult for people in poverty to achieve upward mobility.

modules.title: Race & Ethnicity
modules.objectives: Explain race as a social construction; distinguish prejudice, discrimination, and institutional racism; analyze minority group experiences.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 11: Race and Ethnicity
modules.assignment: Research one historical or contemporary example of institutional racism (e.g., redlining, school funding disparities). Write a 300-word analysis explaining how the policy or practice systematically disadvantages a racial group.

modules.title: Gender & Sexuality
modules.objectives: Distinguish sex from gender; analyze gender socialization and inequality; apply feminist theory; understand LGBTQ+ perspectives sociologically.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 12: Gender, Sex, and Sexuality
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Gender and Sexuality in Society
modules.assignment: Identify two examples of gender socialization from your own life (toys, media, expectations). For each, explain which feminist theory best accounts for it and what change feminist scholars would advocate.

modules.title: Family & Intimate Relationships
modules.objectives: Describe diverse family structures; analyze marriage, divorce, and parenting sociologically; explain how family norms are changing.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 14: Marriage and Family
modules.assignment: Compare the functionalist and conflict theory perspectives on the family. For each perspective, give one strength and one limitation in explaining a current family trend (e.g., rising single-parent households or cohabitation).

modules.title: Education as a Social Institution
modules.objectives: Analyze manifest and latent functions of education; explain how the hidden curriculum reproduces inequality; evaluate educational access gaps.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 16: Education
modules.assignment: Describe two ways the hidden curriculum operates in schools you have attended. Explain who benefits and who is disadvantaged, citing conflict theory in your analysis.

modules.title: Religion & Society
modules.objectives: Apply functionalist, conflict, and symbolic interactionist views to religion; define secularization and fundamentalism; explain religion's role in social change.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 15: Religion
modules.assignment: Choose one historical example where religion drove significant social change (e.g., the Civil Rights Movement, liberation theology, the Reformation). Write a 300-word analysis using at least two sociological perspectives.

modules.title: Political & Economic Institutions
modules.objectives: Define power and authority (Weber's typology); compare types of government; analyze capitalism and socialism using sociological frameworks.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 17: Government and Politics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Political Power and Authority
modules.assignment: Using Weber's three types of authority (traditional, charismatic, rational-legal), identify one real-world leader or institution as an example of each. Explain why each fits that category.

modules.title: Social Change & Global Issues
modules.objectives: Explain theories of social change; analyze social movements; assess globalization and environmental sociology; consider sociology's future directions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e — Ch 21: Social Movements and Social Change
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Social Change and Movements
modules.assignment: Select one contemporary social movement (e.g., climate activism, #MeToo, Black Lives Matter). Apply resource mobilization theory and new social movement theory to explain its rise, tactics, and achievements or limitations.

questions.question: C. Wright Mills defined the 'sociological imagination' as the ability to:
options: Predict future social trends using statistics
See the connections between personal experiences and broader social forces
Create empathy for people of other cultures
Apply psychology to group behavior
questions.answer: See the connections between personal experiences and broader social forces

questions.question: Which theoretical perspective views society as a set of interdependent parts that each serve a function for the whole?
options: Conflict theory
Symbolic interactionism
Functionalism
Feminist theory
questions.answer: Functionalism

questions.question: A sociologist who conducts an ethnography is primarily using which research method?
options: Controlled laboratory experiment
Mailed survey with random sample
Immersive observation and participation in a social setting
Analysis of government census data
questions.answer: Immersive observation and participation in a social setting

questions.question: Informed consent in sociological research means:
options: The researcher informs government agencies of findings
Participants agree to take part after being told the study's purpose and risks
The researcher obtains university approval before publishing
Participants are paid for their involvement
questions.answer: Participants agree to take part after being told the study's purpose and risks

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a cultural norm?
options: The belief that honesty is important
The flag as a national symbol
The practice of shaking hands when greeting someone
The value placed on individual freedom
questions.answer: The practice of shaking hands when greeting someone

questions.question: A counterculture differs from a subculture in that a counterculture:
options: Shares most mainstream values but differs on a few
Actively opposes and challenges mainstream cultural norms and values
Is defined by a shared occupation or hobby
Exists only in online communities
questions.answer: Actively opposes and challenges mainstream cultural norms and values

questions.question: George Herbert Mead's concept of the 'generalized other' refers to:
options: A universal moral code shared across cultures
The internalized sense of society's collective norms and expectations
Any person who does not belong to your primary group
An anonymous stranger in a public space
questions.answer: The internalized sense of society's collective norms and expectations

questions.question: Which agent of socialization is considered the most influential during early childhood?
options: Peer groups
Mass media
The family
Schools
questions.answer: The family

questions.question: An ascribed status is one that is:
options: Earned through personal effort and achievement
Assigned at birth or involuntarily (e.g., race, sex)
Chosen freely by an individual
Temporary and changeable at will
questions.answer: Assigned at birth or involuntarily (e.g., race, sex)

questions.question: Robert Merton's strain theory argues that deviance occurs when:
options: Laws are unclear or inconsistently enforced
People lack the socialized conscience to follow rules
Individuals accept cultural goals but lack legitimate means to achieve them
Social bonds to conventional society weaken
questions.answer: Individuals accept cultural goals but lack legitimate means to achieve them

questions.question: Labeling theory (Howard Becker) focuses on:
options: Biological causes of criminal behavior
How being publicly labeled 'deviant' can become a self-fulfilling prophecy
The role of economic inequality in producing crime
Social bonds that prevent deviance
questions.answer: How being publicly labeled 'deviant' can become a self-fulfilling prophecy

questions.question: Socioeconomic status (SES) is typically measured by a combination of:
options: Race, gender, and age
Income, education, and occupational prestige
Political affiliation, religion, and nationality
Health, happiness, and social connections
questions.answer: Income, education, and occupational prestige

questions.question: In a closed stratification system (e.g., a caste system), social mobility is:
options: Easy and common for those who work hard
Restricted or non-existent; status is fixed at birth
Determined entirely by individual talent
Facilitated by education and marriage
questions.answer: Restricted or non-existent; status is fixed at birth

questions.question: Using the sociological imagination, explain how a 'personal trouble' (such as losing a job) can actually be understood as a 'public issue.' Give a specific example.
questions.answer: Individual unemployment connects to structural forces like recessions, automation, or discriminatory hiring; when many people face the same 'personal' problem simultaneously, it reveals a systemic issue rather than individual failure.

questions.question: Compare and contrast functionalism and conflict theory. How would each perspective explain poverty in society?
questions.answer: Functionalism sees poverty as fulfilling social functions (e.g., motivating work, providing low-wage labor), while conflict theory argues poverty is produced and maintained by powerful groups to exploit the working class. Functionalism stresses social order; conflict theory stresses inequality and power struggles.

questions.question: Symbolic interactionism is most focused on:
options: Large-scale social structures and institutions
The role of economic forces in shaping society
Everyday face-to-face interactions and the meanings people attach to symbols
Biological influences on human behavior
questions.answer: Everyday face-to-face interactions and the meanings people attach to symbols

questions.question: Which research method would be most appropriate for studying the frequency of bullying in a school district?
options: Ethnography
In-depth interviews
Standardized survey administered to all students
Participant observation
questions.answer: Standardized survey administered to all students

questions.question: Cultural relativism is the practice of:
options: Judging other cultures by the standards of your own
Believing your own culture is superior
Evaluating a culture on its own terms without outside judgment
Adopting another culture's practices as your own
questions.answer: Evaluating a culture on its own terms without outside judgment

questions.question: Resocialization — as occurs in total institutions like prisons or the military — primarily involves:
options: Learning a second language
Stripping away old identity and replacing it with a new one suited to the institution
Returning to childhood patterns of behavior
Adopting a new religion
questions.answer: Stripping away old identity and replacing it with a new one suited to the institution

questions.question: A bureaucracy, as described by Max Weber, is characterized by:
options: Informal rules and personal relationships at every level
Hierarchical authority, written rules, specialized roles, and impersonality
Charismatic leadership and flexible decision-making
Democratic voting among all members for every decision
questions.answer: Hierarchical authority, written rules, specialized roles, and impersonality

questions.question: Travis Hirschi's social bond theory argues that deviance results from:
options: Blocked economic opportunities
Being labeled criminal by authorities
Weak ties to conventional social institutions (family, school, community)
Exposure to deviant peers and subcultures
questions.answer: Weak ties to conventional social institutions (family, school, community)

questions.question: Intergenerational mobility refers to:
options: Movement between jobs within a single person's lifetime
A person moving to a new country for better opportunities
Changes in social class between parents and their children
The movement of wealth within a single economic sector
questions.answer: Changes in social class between parents and their children

questions.question: Which of the following best illustrates institutional racism?
options: An individual landlord who refuses to rent to a minority tenant
School funding systems tied to local property taxes that underfund schools in poor, minority neighborhoods
A stereotype held privately by an individual
A cultural preference for one group's food over another's
questions.answer: School funding systems tied to local property taxes that underfund schools in poor, minority neighborhoods

questions.question: The 'glass ceiling' refers to:
options: A transparent barrier in a factory for safety
Invisible barriers that prevent women and minorities from advancing to top leadership positions
A legal limit on executive salaries
Open and transparent corporate governance
questions.answer: Invisible barriers that prevent women and minorities from advancing to top leadership positions

questions.question: Which family form has become most common globally as societies industrialize?
options: Extended family living in multi-generational households
Nuclear family (two parents and children in one household)
Polygamous family with multiple spouses
Communal family with shared child-rearing across the community
questions.answer: Nuclear family (two parents and children in one household)

questions.question: The hidden curriculum in schools refers to:
options: Subjects taught only to advanced students
Unofficial lessons about social norms, obedience, and hierarchy transmitted alongside formal academics
Extracurricular activities and clubs
Secret standardized tests used for tracking students
questions.answer: Unofficial lessons about social norms, obedience, and hierarchy transmitted alongside formal academics

questions.question: Emile Durkheim argued that religion performs which key social function?
options: Suppressing individual freedom in favor of the state
Creating social solidarity by unifying people around shared beliefs and rituals
Generating economic inequality through church ownership of land
Replacing government as the primary authority in society
questions.answer: Creating social solidarity by unifying people around shared beliefs and rituals

questions.question: Max Weber identified three types of authority. Which type is based on established laws and rules rather than personality or tradition?
options: Charismatic authority
Traditional authority
Rational-legal authority
Patriarchal authority
questions.answer: Rational-legal authority

questions.question: Resource mobilization theory explains the success of social movements by emphasizing:
options: The degree of public grievance and emotional outrage
The availability of resources such as money, networks, and organizational skills
The inevitability of historical progress toward equality
Media coverage as the sole driver of change
questions.answer: The availability of resources such as money, networks, and organizational skills

questions.question: Globalization has most directly contributed to:
options: The strengthening of national borders and local economies
Increased interdependence of economies, cultures, and political systems worldwide
Reduced migration between countries
Greater economic equality between rich and poor nations
questions.answer: Increased interdependence of economies, cultures, and political systems worldwide

questions.question: Environmental sociology examines:
options: The biology of ecosystems
The relationship between human social behavior and the natural environment
Government environmental regulations only
Wildlife conservation and biodiversity
questions.answer: The relationship between human social behavior and the natural environment

questions.question: Which concept describes the process by which religious influence declines in modern societies?
options: Fundamentalism
Secularization
Resocialization
Cultural diffusion
questions.answer: Secularization

questions.question: Explain the difference between prejudice and discrimination. Give one example of each and describe how institutional racism differs from individual racism.
questions.answer: Prejudice is a negative attitude or belief about a group; discrimination is an action that treats people unequally based on group membership. Institutional racism refers to policies and structures that systematically disadvantage racial minorities, regardless of individual intent (e.g., discriminatory lending practices), unlike individual racism which involves personal biases and acts.

questions.question: Using at least two sociological concepts from the course, analyze how education both reduces and reproduces social inequality.
questions.answer: Education can reduce inequality by providing credentials and social mobility (meritocracy). However, it also reproduces inequality through tracking/ability grouping (labeling), unequal school funding linked to property taxes (conflict theory), and the hidden curriculum that socializes working-class students toward lower expectations. Cultural capital (Bourdieu) gives middle-class students advantages in academic settings.

questions.question: Describe one social movement from history or the present day. Explain what social change it sought and apply one sociological theory of social movements to analyze its strategy and outcomes.
questions.answer: Acceptable examples include the Civil Rights Movement, #MeToo, or climate activism. Students should identify the movement's goals (e.g., legal equality, corporate accountability, emissions reductions) and apply a theory such as resource mobilization (organizations, funding, media), political opportunity theory (openings in political system), or new social movement theory (identity-based, global, use of social media). Outcomes should be assessed with supporting evidence.

quizQuestions.question: Sociology is best defined as:
options: The study of individual mental processes
The scientific study of society, social behavior, and human groups
The study of political systems and governments
The study of cultural artifacts and art
quizQuestions.answer: The scientific study of society, social behavior, and human groups

quizQuestions.question: The 'sociological imagination' was a term coined by:
options: Emile Durkheim
Max Weber
C. Wright Mills
Karl Marx
quizQuestions.answer: C. Wright Mills

quizQuestions.question: Conflict theory views society as:
options: A stable system where every part serves a purpose
A set of shared symbols and meanings
An arena of competition and struggle over scarce resources and power
A collection of individuals making rational choices
quizQuestions.answer: An arena of competition and struggle over scarce resources and power

quizQuestions.question: Symbolic interactionism focuses on:
options: Large-scale social institutions
How people create meaning through symbols and everyday interactions
Economic inequality between classes
The biological basis of human behavior
quizQuestions.answer: How people create meaning through symbols and everyday interactions

quizQuestions.question: Which sociologist is considered one of the founders of sociology and introduced the concept of 'anomie'?
options: Karl Marx
Max Weber
Auguste Comte
Emile Durkheim
quizQuestions.answer: Emile Durkheim

quizQuestions.question: A hypothesis in sociological research is:
options: A proven fact about social behavior
A testable prediction about the relationship between variables
A summary of findings after a study
A research technique used in fieldwork
quizQuestions.answer: A testable prediction about the relationship between variables

quizQuestions.question: Which research method involves a researcher living among and observing a social group over time?
options: Survey
Experiment
Ethnography
Content analysis
quizQuestions.answer: Ethnography

quizQuestions.question: An independent variable in a sociological study is:
options: The outcome being measured
The factor manipulated or selected to test its effect
The control group's characteristic
A variable that remains constant
quizQuestions.answer: The factor manipulated or selected to test its effect

quizQuestions.question: Reliability in research means:
options: The study accurately measures what it claims to measure
The same results can be consistently replicated
The researcher is personally unbiased
The sample represents the entire population
quizQuestions.answer: The same results can be consistently replicated

quizQuestions.question: Secondary data analysis uses:
options: Data collected fresh by the researcher for the current study
Previously collected data (e.g., government statistics, existing studies)
Only qualitative interview transcripts
Experimental data from lab settings
quizQuestions.answer: Previously collected data (e.g., government statistics, existing studies)

quizQuestions.question: Folkways are best described as:
options: Formal laws with legal penalties for violation
Strong moral norms whose violation causes moral outrage
Everyday customs and conventions that guide behavior
Sacred religious rituals
quizQuestions.answer: Everyday customs and conventions that guide behavior

quizQuestions.question: Mores differ from folkways because mores:
options: Are informal and change rapidly
Are based on strong moral convictions and violation is considered deeply wrong
Apply only to religious communities
Are uniquely modern phenomena
quizQuestions.answer: Are based on strong moral convictions and violation is considered deeply wrong

quizQuestions.question: Ethnocentrism is the tendency to:
options: Study other cultures objectively
Judge other cultures by the standards of your own culture
Adopt the practices of the culture you study
Appreciate the diversity of world cultures
quizQuestions.answer: Judge other cultures by the standards of your own culture

quizQuestions.question: A subculture is a group that:
options: Completely rejects mainstream culture
Shares the dominant culture but maintains distinct practices and values
Exists outside of national borders
Is defined only by racial or ethnic identity
quizQuestions.answer: Shares the dominant culture but maintains distinct practices and values

quizQuestions.question: Cultural diffusion refers to:
options: The breakdown of culture under modernization
The spread of cultural elements from one society to another
The process by which children learn cultural norms
The decline of traditional customs
quizQuestions.answer: The spread of cultural elements from one society to another

quizQuestions.question: The primary socialization an individual receives from family is most important because it:
options: Teaches professional skills
Forms the foundation of language, identity, and basic values
Is legally mandated in most countries
Is reinforced by peers at the same time
quizQuestions.answer: Forms the foundation of language, identity, and basic values

quizQuestions.question: The 'nature vs. nurture' debate in sociology concerns:
options: Whether forests or farmland is more important to society
Whether human behavior is primarily shaped by biology or social environment
Whether individuals or groups matter more in sociological analysis
Whether formal or informal norms are more powerful
quizQuestions.answer: Whether human behavior is primarily shaped by biology or social environment

quizQuestions.question: Anticipatory socialization occurs when:
options: Children learn norms from their parents
An individual practices behaviors and attitudes of a future role they expect to occupy
A person is punished for breaking a norm
An adult relearns norms after entering a new institution
quizQuestions.answer: An individual practices behaviors and attitudes of a future role they expect to occupy

quizQuestions.question: Charles Cooley's 'looking-glass self' concept means:
options: Our self-image is shaped by how we imagine others perceive us
We only value opinions of close family members
Mirrors were culturally important in identity formation
Self-esteem is entirely biological
quizQuestions.answer: Our self-image is shaped by how we imagine others perceive us

quizQuestions.question: Which agent of socialization becomes especially powerful during adolescence?
options: Family
Religion
Peer groups
Government
quizQuestions.answer: Peer groups

quizQuestions.question: A master status is:
options: The highest-ranked person in a social group
The social position that overrides all others and dominates how others see a person
Any status earned through achievement
A temporary role held for a short period
quizQuestions.answer: The social position that overrides all others and dominates how others see a person

quizQuestions.question: Role conflict occurs when:
options: A person fails to perform any of their roles
Two or more of a person's roles have incompatible demands
A social role disappears due to cultural change
A person takes on too many roles simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: Two or more of a person's roles have incompatible demands

quizQuestions.question: Primary groups are characterized by:
options: Large size, formal rules, and impersonal relations
Small size, intimate relationships, and long-term emotional bonds
Shared economic goals and professional networking
Temporary membership based on a shared task
quizQuestions.answer: Small size, intimate relationships, and long-term emotional bonds

quizQuestions.question: A formal organization is different from an informal group because it:
options: Meets in person rather than online
Has explicitly defined goals, rules, and structured positions
Is based entirely on friendship
Has no hierarchy among members
quizQuestions.answer: Has explicitly defined goals, rules, and structured positions

quizQuestions.question: Groupthink is a danger in organizations because it:
options: Encourages too much debate and slows decision-making
Leads members to suppress dissent and make poor collective decisions to maintain consensus
Prevents groups from forming any decision at all
Creates competition that divides the group
quizQuestions.answer: Leads members to suppress dissent and make poor collective decisions to maintain consensus

quizQuestions.question: Deviance is defined sociologically as:
options: Any behavior that is illegal
Behavior that violates widely accepted social norms
Actions that are harmful to individuals
Behavior that is unusual or rare
quizQuestions.answer: Behavior that violates widely accepted social norms

quizQuestions.question: According to Merton's strain theory, 'innovation' as a mode of adaptation means:
options: Accepting both cultural goals and legitimate means
Rejecting both goals and means and withdrawing from society
Accepting cultural goals but using illegitimate means to achieve them
Rejecting cultural goals but conforming to legitimate means
quizQuestions.answer: Accepting cultural goals but using illegitimate means to achieve them

quizQuestions.question: A 'stigma' (as defined by Erving Goffman) is:
options: A medical condition causing social withdrawal
A deeply discrediting attribute that reduces a person from a whole person to a tainted identity
A formal criminal record
A cultural symbol of honor
quizQuestions.answer: A deeply discrediting attribute that reduces a person from a whole person to a tainted identity

quizQuestions.question: Formal social control refers to:
options: Gossip and peer disapproval of rule-breaking
Enforcement of norms by official institutions such as police and courts
Social pressure from friends and family
Internalized conscience developed through socialization
quizQuestions.answer: Enforcement of norms by official institutions such as police and courts

quizQuestions.question: Which statement about deviance is most consistent with a sociological view?
options: Deviance is an objective quality of an act itself
Deviance is biologically determined in certain individuals
Deviance is socially constructed and varies across cultures and time
Deviance only exists in economically disadvantaged communities
quizQuestions.answer: Deviance is socially constructed and varies across cultures and time

quizQuestions.question: Social stratification refers to:
options: Individual differences in personality and ability
The layered ranking of groups in society based on access to wealth, power, and prestige
Geographic differences between regions of a country
Cultural differences between ethnic groups
quizQuestions.answer: The layered ranking of groups in society based on access to wealth, power, and prestige

quizQuestions.question: Absolute poverty is defined as:
options: Earning less than the median income in a society
Lacking resources necessary for basic survival (food, shelter, clean water)
Having fewer resources than one's neighbors
A temporary condition caused by unemployment
quizQuestions.answer: Lacking resources necessary for basic survival (food, shelter, clean water)

quizQuestions.question: The Davis-Moore thesis (functionalist) argues that social inequality:
options: Is inherently unjust and should be eliminated
Serves a function by motivating talented people to fill important positions
Is inevitable but random in who gets rewarded
Is entirely determined by inherited wealth
quizQuestions.answer: Serves a function by motivating talented people to fill important positions

quizQuestions.question: Structural mobility refers to upward or downward movement that results from:
options: Individual hard work and talent
Changes in the economy or occupational structure affecting many people at once
Moving to a new country with better opportunities
Marrying into a different social class
quizQuestions.answer: Changes in the economy or occupational structure affecting many people at once

quizQuestions.question: Which concept captures the total value of a person's assets minus debts?
options: Income
Wealth
Earnings
Profit
quizQuestions.answer: Wealth

quizQuestions.question: Race is considered a social construction because:
options: Racial categories are consistent across all human societies and history
Biological research has definitively identified distinct human races
The categories, meanings, and boundaries of race are created and changed by society, not biology
Race is determined purely by ancestry and genetics
quizQuestions.answer: The categories, meanings, and boundaries of race are created and changed by society, not biology

quizQuestions.question: Prejudice refers to:
options: An action that treats people unequally based on group membership
A preconceived negative attitude or stereotype about a group
A law that discriminates against a minority
A system of unequal power embedded in institutions
quizQuestions.answer: A preconceived negative attitude or stereotype about a group

quizQuestions.question: Genocide is defined as:
options: Forced removal of a group from their homeland
The deliberate and systematic extermination of a racial, ethnic, or national group
Legal segregation of racial groups
Cultural assimilation of minority groups
quizQuestions.answer: The deliberate and systematic extermination of a racial, ethnic, or national group

quizQuestions.question: Which term describes the process by which minority groups adopt the language, norms, and culture of the dominant group?
options: Pluralism
Segregation
Assimilation
Multiculturalism
quizQuestions.answer: Assimilation

quizQuestions.question: White privilege refers to:
options: Wealth accumulated by white families over generations
Active discrimination practiced by white individuals against minorities
Unearned advantages that white people experience by virtue of their race in a racialized society
Legal benefits granted only to white citizens
quizQuestions.answer: Unearned advantages that white people experience by virtue of their race in a racialized society

quizQuestions.question: The sociological distinction between 'sex' and 'gender' is:
options: Sex and gender are the same thing
Sex refers to biological characteristics; gender refers to socially constructed roles and identities
Gender refers to biology; sex refers to social roles
Both are determined entirely by cultural expectations
quizQuestions.answer: Sex refers to biological characteristics; gender refers to socially constructed roles and identities

quizQuestions.question: Gender socialization teaches children:
options: Only academic skills and knowledge
The behaviors, attitudes, and roles considered appropriate for their gender in their society
Their biological sex identity
Economic skills and vocational training
quizQuestions.answer: The behaviors, attitudes, and roles considered appropriate for their gender in their society

quizQuestions.question: Liberal feminism focuses primarily on:
options: Dismantling capitalism as the root of gender oppression
Achieving gender equality through legal reform and equal opportunity policies
Celebrating essential differences between men and women
Eliminating the concept of gender altogether
quizQuestions.answer: Achieving gender equality through legal reform and equal opportunity policies

quizQuestions.question: The gender pay gap refers to:
options: Differences in spending habits between men and women
The average difference in earnings between men and women in the workforce
The cost of women's clothing being higher than men's
Legal salary caps that differ by gender
quizQuestions.answer: The average difference in earnings between men and women in the workforce

quizQuestions.question: Intersectionality (Kimberlé Crenshaw) argues that:
options: Race, gender, and class are each entirely separate and independent systems of oppression
Only race matters for understanding social inequality
Multiple social identities (race, gender, class, sexuality) overlap and interact to shape experience
Gender is the most important axis of inequality
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple social identities (race, gender, class, sexuality) overlap and interact to shape experience

quizQuestions.question: A nuclear family consists of:
options: Multiple generations living together in one household
Two parents and their children living in one household
Any group of people who consider themselves family
A single parent and their children
quizQuestions.answer: Two parents and their children living in one household

quizQuestions.question: Endogamy is the practice of:
options: Marrying outside one's social group
Marrying within one's own social, cultural, or ethnic group
Marrying multiple partners simultaneously
Remaining unmarried throughout one's life
quizQuestions.answer: Marrying within one's own social, cultural, or ethnic group

quizQuestions.question: From a conflict theory perspective, the traditional family structure:
options: Benefits all members equally
Reflects and reinforces gender inequality by assigning women subordinate domestic roles
Is a natural biological arrangement
Exists to promote the emotional well-being of all family members
quizQuestions.answer: Reflects and reinforces gender inequality by assigning women subordinate domestic roles

quizQuestions.question: The divorce rate's rise in the 20th century is associated with:
options: A decline in the popularity of marriage
Greater financial independence of women and changing social norms about staying in unhappy marriages
Stricter divorce laws making it easier to divorce
A decrease in religious participation
quizQuestions.answer: Greater financial independence of women and changing social norms about staying in unhappy marriages

quizQuestions.question: A blended family is defined as:
options: A same-sex couple with adopted children
A family formed when partners with children from previous relationships join together
A family with more than two parents
An extended family network including grandparents and cousins
quizQuestions.answer: A family formed when partners with children from previous relationships join together

quizQuestions.question: A manifest function of education is:
options: Teaching students how to conform to social norms
Providing childcare so parents can work
Transmitting academic knowledge and skills
Creating social networks through peer interaction
quizQuestions.answer: Transmitting academic knowledge and skills

quizQuestions.question: A latent function of education is:
options: Transmitting knowledge and literacy
Providing a marriage market for young adults to meet potential partners
Awarding academic credentials for employment
Following a mandatory curriculum set by the government
quizQuestions.answer: Providing a marriage market for young adults to meet potential partners

quizQuestions.question: Tracking (ability grouping) in schools is criticized by conflict theorists because:
options: It challenges high-achieving students too rigorously
It tends to sort students by race and class, reproducing existing inequalities
It is expensive and difficult to administer
It prevents talented poor students from being identified
quizQuestions.answer: It tends to sort students by race and class, reproducing existing inequalities

quizQuestions.question: Pierre Bourdieu's concept of 'cultural capital' refers to:
options: A nation's investment in arts and museums
Non-financial assets such as education, manners, and knowledge that provide social advantages
Money spent on cultural goods like books and concerts
The economic value of cultural industries
quizQuestions.answer: Non-financial assets such as education, manners, and knowledge that provide social advantages

quizQuestions.question: The credentialism trend means that employers increasingly require:
options: On-the-job experience over formal education
Formal educational credentials (degrees, certificates) even for jobs that don't technically require them
Fewer educational qualifications due to technology replacing skilled work
Only practical skills rather than academic knowledge
quizQuestions.answer: Formal educational credentials (degrees, certificates) even for jobs that don't technically require them

quizQuestions.question: Emile Durkheim argued that religion's primary social function is to:
options: Control individual behavior through fear of divine punishment
Create social cohesion and collective identity through shared rituals
Provide explanations for natural phenomena
Support the economic system by promoting a work ethic
quizQuestions.answer: Create social cohesion and collective identity through shared rituals

quizQuestions.question: From a conflict theory perspective, religion is criticized for:
options: Promoting excessive individualism
Legitimizing and reproducing social inequality ('opiate of the masses' — Marx)
Encouraging critical thinking about social norms
Undermining state authority
quizQuestions.answer: Legitimizing and reproducing social inequality ('opiate of the masses' — Marx)

quizQuestions.question: Secularization refers to:
options: The rise of new religious movements
The decline of religious authority and practice in modern societies
The separation of church and state in law
Conversion from one religion to another
quizQuestions.answer: The decline of religious authority and practice in modern societies

quizQuestions.question: A sect differs from a church in that a sect:
options: Is a larger, more bureaucratic and mainstream religious organization
Is a smaller, more exclusive group that often breaks away from a larger religion and demands strict commitment
Is a government-sponsored religious institution
Has no formal leadership or doctrine
quizQuestions.answer: Is a smaller, more exclusive group that often breaks away from a larger religion and demands strict commitment

quizQuestions.question: Civil religion (Robert Bellah) refers to:
options: Official state-sponsored religious services
A set of quasi-religious beliefs, symbols, and rituals attached to national identity (e.g., pledging allegiance, national holidays)
The merging of multiple religions into one
Religion practiced privately without public expression
quizQuestions.answer: A set of quasi-religious beliefs, symbols, and rituals attached to national identity (e.g., pledging allegiance, national holidays)

quizQuestions.question: Max Weber identified three types of legitimate authority. Traditional authority is based on:
options: Written laws and formal rules
The personal charisma and magnetism of a leader
Long-standing customs and inherited positions (e.g., monarchy)
Popular democratic elections
quizQuestions.answer: Long-standing customs and inherited positions (e.g., monarchy)

quizQuestions.question: A democracy is a political system in which:
options: Power is held by a single ruler with absolute authority
A small elite group makes all major decisions
Political power is derived from and accountable to the people
Religious leaders hold supreme authority
quizQuestions.answer: Political power is derived from and accountable to the people

quizQuestions.question: The power elite theory (C. Wright Mills) argues that:
options: Power is broadly distributed among many competing interest groups
A small, interconnected group of corporate, military, and political leaders dominates decision-making
Economic markets regulate society without the need for political power
Citizens effectively control government through voting and civic participation
quizQuestions.answer: A small, interconnected group of corporate, military, and political leaders dominates decision-making

quizQuestions.question: Capitalism is primarily characterized by:
options: Government ownership of all major industries
Private ownership of the means of production and market-based resource allocation
Equal distribution of wealth and resources
Central planning of the economy by the state
quizQuestions.answer: Private ownership of the means of production and market-based resource allocation

quizQuestions.question: A welfare state combines capitalism with:
options: Military rule
Religious governance
Government programs that guarantee minimum living standards for citizens
A completely free market with no regulation
quizQuestions.answer: Government programs that guarantee minimum living standards for citizens

quizQuestions.question: Social movements are best described as:
options: Spontaneous and unorganized riots
Organized collective efforts to bring about or resist social change
Government-sponsored programs for public improvement
Individual acts of protest against authority
quizQuestions.answer: Organized collective efforts to bring about or resist social change

quizQuestions.question: Resource mobilization theory argues that the success of a social movement depends on:
options: The intensity of public grievance alone
Access to financial resources, organizational networks, and skilled leadership
Government approval of the movement's goals
The number of supporters willing to use violence
quizQuestions.answer: Access to financial resources, organizational networks, and skilled leadership

quizQuestions.question: Globalization has contributed to cultural homogenization, which means:
options: Greater respect for cultural differences worldwide
The spread of a dominant culture (often Western) that reduces global cultural diversity
The revival of local traditions in response to foreign influence
Equal exchange of cultural practices between all societies
quizQuestions.answer: The spread of a dominant culture (often Western) that reduces global cultural diversity

quizQuestions.question: The treadmill of production concept in environmental sociology argues that:
options: Technology will solve all environmental problems
Capitalism's drive for growth inherently conflicts with environmental sustainability
Pollution is caused primarily by individual consumer choices
Environmental problems are exaggerated by the media
quizQuestions.answer: Capitalism's drive for growth inherently conflicts with environmental sustainability

quizQuestions.question: New social movements (e.g., environmental, feminist, LGBTQ+ movements) differ from older labor movements because they primarily focus on:
options: Economic wages and working conditions
Identity, recognition, and quality-of-life issues rather than solely economic class interests
Overthrowing existing governments by force
Gaining control of major industries
quizQuestions.answer: Identity, recognition, and quality-of-life issues rather than solely economic class interests
